Game.brief: A static utility class that manages core game state, configuration, and initialization routines for the server.
Game.details: The Game class functions as a centralized static utility container that maintains the global state, configuration settings, and operational flags of the game server. It provides access to the singleton World instance through the static method world(), enabling consistent interaction with the game environment. The class encapsulates various static variables such as network port, message of the day (motd), help greeting, and flags indicating server status like booting, wizlock, and newlock. Additionally, it tracks important gameplay statistics including total logins, maximum concurrent players, and peak player counts since last boot. Designed as a non-instantiable class, it employs a private constructor and deleted copy operations to enforce static usage only. The boot() method initializes all essential game components, ensuring the server is ready for operation. Its virtual destructor guarantees proper cleanup in inheritance scenarios, although instantiation of the class is intentionally restricted to static context, emphasizing its role as a namespace for global game data and functions.
Game.notes: The Game class employs a singleton-like pattern for the World instance and uses static members exclusively, reflecting its role as a global state manager. It is designed to prevent instantiation and copying, ensuring a single source of truth for game-wide configurations and statistics.
Game.rationale: This class exists to provide a centralized, easily accessible interface for managing the core state and configuration of the game server. By consolidating global settings, status flags, and statistics, it simplifies system coordination, facilitates consistent behavior across components, and supports controlled initialization and shutdown procedures within the system architecture.
Game.name: Game

port.brief: The `Game::port` variable stores the network port number used for the game server connection.
port.details: This static integer represents the port on which the game server listens for incoming network connections. It is typically initialized from environment variables such as DIZZYPORT or overridden by command-line arguments during server startup, ensuring flexible configuration. The variable is referenced in multiple parts of the codebase to set up socket connections, enforce environment-specific command restrictions, and preserve port consistency during server restarts or resets. Its value plays a crucial role in controlling server communication, stall detection, and cleanup operations, thereby supporting stable and predictable server behavior.
port.notes: As a static variable, `Game::port` maintains a single shared value across all instances, emphasizing its role in global server configuration. Proper initialization and updates are essential to prevent misconfigurations or conflicts during server lifecycle events.
port.rationale: This variable exists to centralize the management of the server's network port, enabling consistent configuration and behavior across different components and operational scenarios, including restarts and environment-specific restrictions.
port.name: port

motd.brief: A static string that stores the current message of the day (MOTD) for the game.
motd.details: The `motd` variable holds the message of the day displayed to players upon login or at specific intervals. Being a static member of the `Game` class, it is shared across all instances and accessible globally. It is typically initialized during server startup or updated dynamically through administrative commands. The `String` class used allows for flexible string operations, including case-insensitive comparisons, which facilitate message handling and display logic throughout the codebase. This variable is referenced in various functions responsible for presenting the MOTD to players and managing its content.
motd.notes: As a static variable, `motd` is stored once per program execution, ensuring consistent access across different parts of the server. Its mutable nature allows for runtime updates, enabling administrators to change the MOTD without restarting the server.
motd.rationale: The `motd` variable exists to provide a centralized, easily accessible message that can inform or motivate players, ensuring consistent communication across the game environment. Its static nature supports global access and easy updates, which are essential for dynamic messaging scenarios.
motd.name: motd

usages.wiz__gen_8cc, void do_motd(Character *ch: Global variable holding the current message of the day, which is read, modified, or cleared based on the argument, and displayed to the character.
usages.structconn_1_1_read_m_o_t_d_state, State: Checked for emptiness to determine whether to display the message of the day to the character.

help_greeting.brief: A static string that stores the default greeting message shown to new players and descriptors.
help_greeting.details: The `help_greeting` variable is a static member of the `Game` class that holds the message used as a greeting when a new descriptor connects to the game. It is initialized during game startup, either from a database query result or set to a default message if no data is available. This greeting is used in functions like `init_descriptor` to determine what message to send to players upon connection. Additionally, it can be dynamically updated through help management functions such as `add_help` when the 'GREETING' keyword is specified, allowing for flexible customization of the initial message. As a static variable, it ensures a consistent greeting across all instances and sessions within the game.
help_greeting.notes: Since `help_greeting` is static, it maintains a single shared message for all descriptors. It is typically set during initialization and can be modified at runtime, providing a centralized way to manage the game's introductory message.
help_greeting.rationale: This variable exists to provide a customizable and consistent initial message to players, enhancing the onboarding experience and allowing administrators to easily update the greeting without altering core code.
help_greeting.name: help_greeting

booting.brief: A static boolean flag indicating whether the game is currently in the booting (initialization) phase.
booting.details: The `Game::booting` variable serves as a global indicator of the game's startup state. It is set to true at the beginning of the boot process and switched to false once initialization completes. This flag is crucial for controlling behavior during startup, such as suppressing error reports for missing prototypes, managing database connections, and preventing side effects during object creation. Its static scope ensures that all parts of the codebase have a consistent understanding of whether the game is still initializing, enabling conditional logic that depends on the booting status.
booting.notes: The variable is typically toggled at the start and end of the boot sequence, and its static nature allows for easy access across different modules without requiring object instances.
booting.rationale: This variable exists to coordinate startup procedures and prevent certain actions from executing prematurely, ensuring a smooth and error-free initialization process.
booting.name: booting

current_time.brief: A static variable that holds the current system time in epoch format within the Game namespace.
current_time.details: Game::current_time is a static time_t variable used throughout the game codebase to represent the current system time in epoch seconds. It is initialized at startup, often by calling system functions like gettimeofday, and is regularly updated during the game loop to maintain an accurate measure of real-world time. This variable plays a critical role in various functionalities, including generating unique player IDs based on the current time, scheduling reboots, and performing timing calculations for game events. Its consistent updating ensures synchronized time-dependent operations across different game components.
current_time.notes: Since it is a static variable, Game::current_time is shared across all instances and functions within the namespace, serving as a global reference for the current time. Proper synchronization and timely updates are essential to prevent discrepancies in time-sensitive features.
current_time.rationale: The variable exists to provide a centralized, easily accessible reference to the current system time, enabling consistent timing logic and coordination across different parts of the game system, especially for operations that depend on real-time data.
current_time.name: current_time

log_all.brief: A static flag that indicates whether all commands should be logged globally.
log_all.details: The `Game::log_all` variable is a static boolean used to control the global logging state within the game. When set to true, it ensures that every command issued by any character is logged, regardless of individual command settings or preferences. This flag is typically toggled via functions like `do_log` with the 'all' argument, providing a convenient way to enable or disable comprehensive command logging for debugging or monitoring purposes. It is initialized at program startup and remains active until explicitly changed, maintaining consistent logging behavior throughout the game session.
log_all.notes: As a static member, `log_all` maintains its state across all instances and functions, serving as a central control point for logging behavior.
log_all.rationale: This variable exists to facilitate comprehensive logging during development or troubleshooting, allowing developers to monitor all command activity without modifying individual command configurations.
log_all.name: log_all

usages.interp_8cc, void interpret(Character *ch: Global flag that, if set, causes all commands to be logged regardless of individual command settings.
usages.wiz__secure_8cc, void do_log(Character *: Toggled when the argument is 'all', controlling whether all logging is enabled or disabled globally.

record_logins.brief: A static variable that maintains the total number of login records processed during the application's runtime.
record_logins.details: The `record_logins` variable is a static member of the `Game` class, serving as a centralized counter for all login events. It is initialized during the startup sequence of the application, often set based on data retrieved from persistent storage in the `boot()` method. Throughout the application's operation, it is incremented each time a login is successfully recorded, such as within the `conn::update_records()` function. This variable provides a consistent and accessible tally of login activity, facilitating monitoring and analytics related to user authentication events.
record_logins.notes: Since `record_logins` is static, it retains its value across all instances of the `Game` class and throughout the application's lifetime. Proper synchronization is assumed in multi-threaded contexts if applicable, although not explicitly shown here.
record_logins.rationale: This variable exists to track total login activity efficiently, enabling features such as usage statistics, security audits, and ensuring accurate record-keeping across the application's runtime.
record_logins.name: record_logins

usages.class_game, void Game::boot(): Set from database query result to record the number of logins during boot.
usages.namespaceconn, unsigned long conn::updat: Incremented at the end of the function to update the total number of logins recorded.

record_players.brief: Tracks the highest number of players simultaneously recorded during the game session.
record_players.details: The `record_players` variable maintains the maximum number of players that have been connected to the game at the same time throughout its runtime. It is initialized during the startup process, typically based on data retrieved from persistent storage or database queries. The variable is updated dynamically whenever the current player count surpasses its previous value, ensuring it always reflects the all-time peak. It is accessed in various functions, such as status displays, record updates, and during the boot process to set initial values from stored data.
record_players.notes: Since `record_players` is a static variable, it retains its value across different instances and parts of the program, serving as a global record within the `Game` class context.
record_players.rationale: This variable exists to monitor and record the maximum concurrency level of players, enabling features like displaying historical high scores, triggering events when new records are set, or maintaining statistics for game analytics.
record_players.name: record_players

usages.class_game, void Game::boot(): Set from database query result to record the number of players during boot.

record_players_since_boot.brief: Stores the highest number of players connected to the system since the last boot.
record_players_since_boot.details: The `record_players_since_boot` variable keeps track of the maximum number of players that have been connected to the system since it was last started or rebooted. It is initialized during system startup and is updated whenever a new peak in player connections is detected, such as during connection handling or record updating routines. This variable is essential for monitoring system load, generating statistics on peak activity, and supporting features that display or reset maximum player counts. It interacts with connection management functions and record-keeping routines to ensure the stored value accurately reflects the highest concurrent player count over the current session.
record_players_since_boot.notes: Typically stored as a static integer to persist across function calls within the system's runtime. Its updates are synchronized with connection events to maintain an accurate peak record.
record_players_since_boot.rationale: This variable exists to facilitate tracking of peak player activity since the last system reboot, enabling administrators and systems to analyze load patterns and manage resources effectively.
record_players_since_boot.name: record_players_since_boot

quest_double.brief: A static integer flag within the `Game` class that indicates whether quest rewards or points are doubled.
quest_double.details: The `quest_double` variable is a static member of the `Game` class used to signal when players should receive double quest rewards or points. It is typically set to a non-zero value during special events or conditions that activate double rewards, and reset to zero when the effect concludes. The variable is checked in multiple functions such as `do_quest`, `do_time`, and `age_update` to modify game behavior accordingly, ensuring that the double reward effect is consistently applied across different game mechanics.
quest_double.notes: Since `quest_double` is a static flag, it maintains its state across different parts of the game without requiring instance references. Proper management of its value is essential to prevent unintended double reward activation outside designated periods.
quest_double.rationale: This variable exists to facilitate temporary game events that incentivize players by doubling quest rewards, thereby enhancing engagement and providing controlled timing for such effects.
quest_double.name: quest_double

usages.quest_8cc, void do_quest(Character *ch,: Checked to determine if double rewards are active during quest completion.
usages.act__info_8cc, void do_time(Character *c: Checked to determine if quest points are being doubled; if true, triggers the display of the double points message.
usages.update_8cc, void age_update(): Incremented if non-zero, likely to double quest-related events or timers during the function execution.

wizlock.brief: A static boolean flag indicating whether the game is currently in wizard lock mode.
wizlock.details: The `wizlock` variable is a static member of the `Game` class that indicates whether the game is in a locked state, restricting access to certain features for non-immortal players. It is typically initialized to false and toggled by administrative commands such as `do_wizlock`. The variable is checked in various parts of the code, including input handling routines like `HandleInput`, to enforce access restrictions when the game is in wizard lock mode. This mechanism helps maintain control during maintenance or administrative tasks by preventing regular players from interacting with the game when locked.
wizlock.notes: Since `wizlock` is a static variable, it maintains its state across different instances and parts of the program, ensuring consistent access control throughout the game session.
wizlock.rationale: The variable exists to provide a centralized and easily accessible flag for enabling or disabling game access for non-privileged players, supporting administrative control and game stability during critical operations.
wizlock.name: wizlock

usages.wiz__coder_8cc, void do_wizlock(Characte: Accessed and modified as a global variable to toggle the game's wizlock state, controlling whether the game is locked or unlocked.
usages.structconn_1_1_get_name_state, State * c: Checked to restrict access to non-immortal players when the game is in wizard lock mode, controlling access permissions.

newlock.brief: A static boolean flag that indicates whether the creation of new characters is currently locked out.
newlock.details: The `Game::newlock` variable is a static boolean within the Game namespace that signifies whether new character creation is temporarily disabled. When set to true, it prevents players from creating new characters by influencing related functions such as `do_newlock`, which toggles this flag, and connection handling routines that check this flag to decide whether to accept or reject new connection attempts. Its value is initialized and modified elsewhere in the codebase, and it plays a key role in managing server state during lockout periods, including broadcasting appropriate messages or closing connections to enforce the lockout.
newlock.notes: This flag is typically checked during connection handling and character creation processes to enforce lockout policies efficiently.
newlock.rationale: The variable exists to provide a centralized control point for temporarily disabling new character creation, supporting server administrators in managing player influx during maintenance, updates, or other restricted periods.
newlock.name: newlock

usages.wiz__secure_8cc, void do_newlock(Charact: Accessed and modified as a static variable within the Game namespace to toggle the state of new character lockout, and its new value determines which messages are broadcasted and sent.
usages.structconn_1_1_get_name_state, State * c: Checked to determine if new player creation is temporarily disabled, leading to connection closure if true.

virtual Game::~Game().brief: Virtual destructor for the Game class to ensure proper cleanup of resources in derived classes.
virtual Game::~Game().details: This destructor is declared virtual to enable correct destruction of objects through base class pointers, which is essential in polymorphic class hierarchies. Although the destructor body is empty in the base class, its virtual nature guarantees that the destructors of any derived classes are called appropriately, allowing for proper resource deallocation and cleanup. This approach prevents resource leaks and maintains the integrity of the object lifecycle, especially when subclasses manage additional resources or perform specific cleanup tasks.
virtual Game::~Game().returns: This function does not return a value.
virtual Game::~Game().notes: Having a virtual destructor in the base class is a common best practice in C++ when dealing with inheritance, ensuring that destructors of derived classes are invoked correctly during object destruction.
virtual Game::~Game().rationale: This destructor is used whenever a Game object or its derived classes are deleted through a base class pointer, ensuring that all resources are properly released and preventing potential resource leaks in polymorphic scenarios.
virtual Game::~Game().name: ~Game

World & Game::world().brief: Provides access to the singleton instance of the game world, ensuring a single shared environment throughout the game.
World & Game::world().details: This function employs the singleton pattern by returning a static instance of the World class, which is initialized on the first call and remains persistent for the duration of the program. It guarantees that all modules and components interact with the same game world state, including areas, characters, and environmental data. This centralized access simplifies management and synchronization of game state, enabling consistent behavior across various parts of the codebase that require interaction with the game environment.
World & Game::world().returns: A reference to the singleton World instance used throughout the game.
World & Game::world().notes: Since the World instance is static within the function, it is initialized only once, making this method thread-safe in C++11 and later standards. This design ensures that all references to the game world are consistent and up-to-date.
World & Game::world().rationale: Used whenever parts of the game need to access or modify the global game environment, such as retrieving character lists, environmental states, or spatial data, ensuring a unified and synchronized game state.
World & Game::world().name: world

void Game::boot().brief: Initializes and loads all essential game components during startup.
void Game::boot().details: The Game::boot function manages the full initialization sequence of the game environment. It begins by setting a flag indicating the system is in the booting phase and initializes the random number generator to ensure variability in game mechanics. It then verifies the integrity of skill and affect tables by checking that all enum entries have corresponding definitions, logging bugs and terminating if inconsistencies are detected. The function confirms the validity of the game world object, loads core data such as clan information, and initializes the auction system. It proceeds to load all game areas, create room instances, and establish room exits, preparing the environment for gameplay. Afterward, it initializes quest data and loads game objects from disk, including war, arena, notes, disabled commands, songs, social interactions, storage, and departed player lists. The world state is then updated, and various game statistics are retrieved from the database, such as login and player counts. It also loads the greeting message for new players from the database, defaulting to a preset message if necessary. Throughout the process, progress messages are outputted for debugging purposes, ensuring each step completes successfully before proceeding. Finally, it marks the booting process as complete, updates the world, and sets trigger flags to enable game interactions.
void Game::boot().returns: This function does not return a value.
void Game::boot().throws: The function may terminate the program if critical data verification fails or if database queries for essential information (such as greeting message) fail.
void Game::boot().notes: The function performs multiple critical startup tasks sequentially, including data validation, environment setup, and resource loading. It relies on database queries for configuration data and ensures that all game components are correctly initialized before gameplay begins. The use of progress messages aids in debugging and monitoring startup success.
void Game::boot().rationale: This function is invoked during the initial startup of the game to ensure all systems, data, and environment components are properly loaded and validated, providing a stable foundation for gameplay and system operations.
void Game::boot().name: boot

usages.comm_8cc, int main(int argc, char **argv: Called to initialize and load essential game components during startup, signaling the start of the boot process.

Game::Game().brief: Constructs a new instance of the Game class, primarily for internal use or static initialization.
Game::Game().details: This constructor initializes a new Game object but is mainly used internally within the class or during static setup. Since the class is designed as a static class with a private constructor, this function does not perform any resource allocation or complex setup. Its primary purpose is to satisfy language requirements and facilitate internal mechanisms, ensuring controlled instantiation of the class.
Game::Game().throws: This constructor does not throw exceptions.
Game::Game().notes: The constructor is private or protected, emphasizing that instantiation is controlled and typically not used directly by external code.
Game::Game().rationale: Used internally or during static initialization to ensure proper setup of the Game class, which is designed to be used as a static class with no external instantiation.
Game::Game().name: Game

Game::Game(const Game &).brief: Copy constructor for the Game class.
Game::Game(const Game &).details: Creates a new instance of the Game class by copying the state of an existing Game object. This constructor duplicates the internal state of the provided Game object, ensuring that the new instance is a separate copy with the same initial state.

params.Game&: A reference to the Game object to be copied.

Game::Game(const Game &).returns: This constructor does not return a value as it is used to initialize a new instance of the Game class.
Game::Game(const Game &).name: Game

Game & Game::operator=(const Game &).brief: Assigns the state of another Game object to this one.
Game & Game::operator=(const Game &).details: This copy assignment operator allows one Game object to be assigned the state of another. It performs a deep copy of the data from the source Game object to the destination Game object, ensuring that both objects are independent of each other after the assignment. This operator handles self-assignment and ensures that any dynamically allocated resources are properly managed to prevent memory leaks.

params.rhs: The source Game object whose state is to be copied.

Game & Game::operator=(const Game &).returns: A reference to this Game object after assignment.
Game & Game::operator=(const Game &).name: operator=
